content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gregory G. Diaz 
City Attorney 
50 l Poli Street 
Ventura, CA 93002 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-181 
Dear Mr. Diaz: 
September 21, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Buenaventura City 
Councilmember Cheryl Heitmann regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 
l FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that 
may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective 
actions. 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Heitmann make and participate in decisions on a moratorium 
ordinance pertaining to short-term vacation rentals in the city? 
CONCLUSION 
Councilmember Heitmann may not participate in city council consideration of decisions on 
a moratorium ordinance pertaining to short-term vacation rentals in the city. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-181 
Page No. 2 
The City of San Buenaventura is a charter city located along the coast in Ventura County. 
The population of the City is approximately I 08,000 and there are approximately 45,000 housing 
units in the City. 
The City first began regulating short-term vacation rentals ("STVR") in 2007 through a 
licensing and permit ordinance. The City issues licenses to qualified applicants at qualified 
properties through the City's Finance and Technology Department. As of the date of this letter, the 
City has approximately 112 STVR permits issued city-wide. 
With the advent of on-line websites that allow advertising and scheduling of STVR, the 
number of such pennits has been growing over the years. The City Council at its meeting of 
July 25, 2016, approved a Policy Consideration 1 (PC-1) directing the City Attorney to prepare a 
moratorium ordinance on STVR permits and directed City staff to investigate options being 
undertaken by other communities to involve the public in the decision-making process and what 
types of regulations other communities in the state have adopted. PC-I is to be brought back to the 
City Council at its meeting of September 26, 2016 for consideration. 
You noted that the Policy Consideration is the process used in the City for individual City 
Council members to have projects added to the agenda and for City staff to spend personnel and 
other resources on it. A simple majority of the City Council approves a Policy Consideration and 
work begins thereafter. While it is clear that the City Council has directed staff to prepare a 
moratorium, what is not clear is what will happen once the public process to detennine the future of 
STVRs will be. It is possible that the City Council could agree to leave the existing regulations 
unchanged, could leave the existing regulations unchanged but step up enforcement, could add 
additional or stronger regulations to what is already in the City's Municipal Code, could impose 
some zoning requirements, or could simply discontinue the issuance of further permits for new 
STVRs. If this is the case, it will be necessary for the City Council to determine what to do with 
existing STVRs. 
The Pic1po11I Commu11ity: The vast majority of the STVRs are in the Pierpont community 
along the residential section of the City's coastline. You noted that property values in the Pierpont 
area have increased as have most coastal properties in California, however, you have no evidence to 
suggest that properties have increased more or less in Pierpont due to the ability to utilize the homes 
as STVRs if a permit is obtained from the city. You also stated that you have heard arguments both 
that permitting STVRs ( 1) increases value in properties because there is a potential revenue stream, 
and (2) there is a decrease in property values caused by STVRs given the nature of the use and the 
fact that some of the guests at the STVRs are less than courteous to their neighbors. You stated that 
you have no empirical evidence to provide one way or the other on this issue. 
Moreover, while there are STVRs throughout the City, the issues and complaints regarding 
this use of property appear to emanate largely from the Pierpont neighborhood. However, 
complaints regarding the use have not been limited to just the Pierpont community. Because the 
usage is allowed citywide subject to a permit, the City Council wishes to look at the entire 
community in reaching the appropriate policy decision rather than one particular sub-community or 
neighborhood. 


File No. A-16-181 
Page No. 3 
Councilmember Heitmann: City Councilmember Cheryl Heitmann was first elected to the 
City Council in 2009. She owns a residence in the city with her husband. The property is owned in 
fee. Their residence is in the Pierpont community in the coastal section of the City of San 
Buenaventura. Within a 500-foot radius of her residence, there are currently nine STVR permits. In 
addition, there are six to ten additional permits issued in relatively close proximity to the 500-foot 
radius. Councilmember Heitmann does not use her property as a STVR. She has expressed no 
interest in doing so as it is her personal residence. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official, the official's financial interests, or on the official's 
immediate family that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Under 
Sections 82033 and 87103(b ), an official has an interest in any real property owned directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the interest has a 
fair market value of $2,000 or more. The Councilmember's only interest implicated by the facts 
presented is her real property interests in her residence. 
Fol'eseeability a11d Materiality 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision 
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I )-(6). 
Councilmember Heitmann does not use her property as a STVR. She has expressed no interest in 
doing so as it is her personal residence. And while theoretically affected by the such an ordinance, it 
would not constitute the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the councilmember. 
In all other cases, Regulation 1870 I (b) provides the standard of foreseeability. Under that 
provision, a financial effect on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical; a financial effect need 
not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. 
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest and the level of the 
interest's involvement. Specific provisions implicated by your facts are: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 


File No. A-16-181 
Page No. 4 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
As noted above, Councilrnember Heitmann does not use her property as a STVR and she 
has expressed no interest in doing so. However, changing the use of property within close proximity 
to her real property interest could affect her property's value under the three provisions set forth 
above. 
Under your facts, the moratorium could have a more significant impact on the Pierpont 
region of the city where STVR permits are overwhelming concentrated. Future permit requests 
would likely continue to follow that pattern of accumulating in the Pierpont region. 
Moreover, the current posture of the decision could also include the possibility that the 
status of existing STY Rs be reevaluated or changed. Elimination of the nine STVR permitted 
properties within 500 feet of her home (or even implementing further regulation) could substantially 
alter the nature of the councilmember's neighborhood and could cause a reasonably prudent person, 
using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the 
councilmember's property. 
Consequently, the facts provided are insufficient to indicate that there will be no reasonably 
foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property, and a financial effect on the 
councilmember's property resulting from the moratorium is foreseeable and material. 
Public Generally Exceptio11 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: "A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant 


File No. A-16-181 
Page No. 5 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique 
compared to the effect on the significant segment." 
For purposes of your question, Regulation 18703(b) states that a significant segment of the 
public is at least 25 percent of: 
• 
All real property within the official's jurisdiction; 
• 
All commercial real property within the official's jurisdiction or 
• 
All residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. 
Regulation I 8703(c) states that a unique effect on a public official's financial interest 
includes a disproportionate effect on: 
"(l )  The development potential or use of the official's real property or on 
the income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting 
from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially !:,'Teater than the effect on a single interest. 
"( 4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting 
from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property 
if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate 
family." 
Significant Segment: Of the 45,000 housing units in the City, only 112 housing units are 
permitted for use as a STVR. This is less that 1 percent of the housing units in the city. As noted 
above, Councilmember Heitmann does not use her property as a STVR and she has expressed no 
interest in doing so. The effect on her financial interest is based on her proximity to these STVRs. 
However, it remains questionable that even considering all housing units in close proximity to an 
STVR (and most likely impacted by the STVR use) that 25 percent of the residential real properties 
in the city would be affected. However, we need not make a definitive finding with respect to the 
significant segment. 
Unique Effects: The Councilmember's property is in an area, the Pierpont community, in 
which STVR's are concentrated. You also stated that the issues and complaints regarding this use of 
property appear to emanate largely from the Pierpont neighborhood. Of the 112 permits citywide, 


File No. A-16-181 
Page No. 6 
you noted that nine are within 500 feet of the councilmember's property and including those nine, 
approximately 19 are within, or in relatively close proximity to, the 500-foot radius. Having 19 
STVR permitted properties in close proximity would suggest that any changes to the treatment of 
STVRs in the city would uniquely affect the councilmember's property as compared to other 
residential properties within the city as a whole. 
In this case, because the STVR's are highly concentrated in the Pierpont community, the 
public generally exception would not apply unless the Pierpont community made up 25 percent of 
the residential real properties within the city. Since it does not appear that the Pierpont community 
constitutes a significant segment, the exception would not apply and the councilmember would 
have a conflict of interest. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
/ 
By: 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
. Wallace 
General Counsel 
IVISIOn
